title: Classes and Objects
description: Model the real world in your code.

slides.title: The Next Level 🏆
slides.content:
## Object-Oriented Programming

Until now, we've used basic Data Types like `Int` and `String`. But what if you are building a game and need to represent a **User**?

A User isn't just a string. A User has a Name (String), an Age (Int), an Email (String), and an Avatar (Image). 

We need a way to group these together into a single concept.

slides.title: The Blueprint 📐
slides.content:
To invent our own data type, we create a **Class**.

A Class is a blueprint. It describes what a "User" *should* look like, but it is not an actual user itself.

Think of a Class like the architectural blueprint for a house. You can't live inside a blueprint!

quizData.question: What is the best analogy for a Class in programming?

options.text: A physical house you can walk into.
A blueprint mapping out how a house should be built.
A function that generates a house.

quizData.explanation: A class strictly acts as the definitive design template. It's the set of rules for constructing the actual thing later.

slides.title: Creating Objects 🏗️
slides.content:
When we take the Class blueprint and use it to literally build something in the computer's memory, the result is called an **Object** (or an Instance).

You use the class name followed by parentheses `()` to build a new Object.

quizData.question: What happens when you write `User()` with the parentheses?

options.text: It calls a function called User.
It constructs a brand-new Object in memory based on the User blueprint.

quizData.explanation: Calling the class name with parentheses tells Kotlin exactly what to 'manufacture'. Let's build a new item from this set of plans!
